perturbation.original_text: Information" shall for the purpose of this agreement include, without limitation, any technical, commercial, scientific information, know -how, trade secrets, processes, machinery, designs, drawings, technical spe cifications, data in whatever form and information referred to in clause 1 (introduction) hereinabove communicated to the receiving party or acquired by the receiving party from the disclosing party during the course of the parties' association with one an other.
perturbation.changed_text: "Information" shall for the purpose of this agreement include only explicit, written, and directly labeled 'Confidential' data. This excludes all implied, oral, or indirectly related data, regardless of its nature or origin.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of 'Information' significantly narrows the scope of confidential data protected by the NDA, potentially excluding crucial information considered trade secrets under South African law. The original text included 'any technical, commercial, scientific information, know-how, trade secrets, processes, machinery, designs, drawings, technical specifications, data in whatever form', so limiting it introduces a misalignment with the broad protection intended for trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the broad protection afforded to trade secrets under South African law, particularly Section 22(1)(a) of the Competition Act, which addresses the unauthorized disclosure of confidential information. By limiting the definition of 'Information' to only explicitly labeled data, it fails to protect unlabelled but confidential information that can still be considered a trade secret.
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of 'Information' to only 'explicit, written, and directly labeled 'Confidential' data', excluding 'implied, oral, or indirectly related data'. This contradicts the Law Explanation, which states this fails to protect unlabelled but confidential information that can still be considered a trade secret under South African law, particularly Section 22(1)(a) of the Competition Act, which addresses the unauthorized disclosure of confidential information. While there are no law snippets to reinforce the conclusion, the Perturbation Explanation emphasizes the original contract's broader scope ('any technical, commercial, scientific information, know-how, trade secrets, processes, machinery, designs, drawings, technical specifications, data in whatever form') and how the modification weakens the protection of trade secrets, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute arising out of or in connection with the provisions of this agreement shall be referred to arbitration and determined in accordance with the Arbitration Act 42 of 1965, as amended from time to time,
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute arising out of or in connection with the provisions of this agreement shall be resolved through public court proceedings, waiving all rights to arbitration, mediation, or any alternative dispute resolution methods.
perturbation.explanation: The modification forces disputes into public court, directly contradicting the Arbitration Act 42 of 1965, which promotes and regulates arbitration as a means of dispute resolution. By waiving all rights to arbitration, the clause violates the Act's provisions for enforcing arbitration agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 3 of the Arbitration Act 42 of 1965 provides that arbitration agreements are generally binding and enforceable unless there is sufficient cause to the contrary. The modified text completely negates this principle by mandating public court proceedings and explicitly waiving any form of arbitration, contradicting the Act's core purpose of promoting and facilitating arbitration.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 9-307. LOCATION OF DEBTOR. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 9-307. LOCATION OF DEBTOR. (a) ["Place of business."] In this section, "place of business" means a place where a debtor conducts its affairs. (b) [Debtor's location: general rules.] Except as otherwise provided in this section, the following rules determine a debtor 's location: (1) A debtor who is an individual is located at the individual's principal residence. (2) A debtor that is an organization and has only one place of business is located at its place of business. (3) A debtor that is an organization and has more than one place of business is located at its chief executive office. (c) [Limitation of applicability of subsection (b).] Subsection (b) applies only if a debtor 's residence, place of business, or chief executive office, as applicable, is located in a jurisdiction whose law generally requires information concerning the existence of a nonpossessory security interest to be made generally available in a filing, recording, or registration system as a condition or result of the security interest's obtaining priority over the rights of a lien creditor with respect to the collateral. If subsection (b) does not apply, the debtor is located in the District of Columbia. (d) [Continuation of location: cessation of existence, etc.] A person that ceases to exist, have a residence, or have a place of business continues to be located in the jurisdiction specified by subsections (b) and (c). (e) [Location of registered organization organized under State law.] A registered organization that is organized under the law of a State is located in that State. (f) [Location of registered organization organized under federal law; bank branches and agencies.] Except as otherwise provided in subsection (i), a registered organization that is organized under the law of the United States and a branch or agency of a bank that is not organized under the law of the United States or a State are located: (1) in the State that the law of the United States designates, if the law designates a State of location; (2) in the State that the registered organization , branch, or agency designates, if the law of the United States authorizes the registered organization, branch, or agency to designate its State of location, including by designating its main office, home office, or other comparable office; or (3) in the District of Columbia, if neither paragraph (1) nor paragraph (2) applies. (g) [Continuation of location: change in status of registered organizatio
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates public court proceedings and waives all rights to arbitration, directly contradicting the purpose and enforcement of arbitration agreements. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that Section 3 of the Arbitration Act 42 of 1965 makes arbitration agreements binding unless there is a valid reason otherwise. The modified text explicitly negates this principle. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address arbitration, the Law Explanation references the relevant section of the Arbitration Act which is contradicted by the modified text.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement and the relationship of the parties in connection with the subject matter of this agreement shall be governed and determined in accordance with the laws of 8 South Africa.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement and the relationship of the parties shall be governed by the laws of the United States, specifically Delaware, irrespective of the physical location of assets or business operations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from South Africa to Delaware (USA) disregards the principle of *lex loci contractus* and *lex situs*, which often dictates that contracts related to property or business within a jurisdiction are governed by that jurisdiction's laws. This can conflict with South African legal principles regarding contractual obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Private International Law (Conflict of Laws)
perturbation.law_citation: Choice of law rules in South African contract law
perturbation.law_explanation: South African private international law contains choice of law rules that determine which legal system should govern a contract with international elements. While parties can choose a governing law, South African courts may disregard such a choice if it's made in bad faith or undermines fundamental South African public policy or mandatory rules that apply to the substance of the transaction. By forcing Delaware law onto a contract centered in South Africa, especially regarding assets located there, this change risks conflict with established South African conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces South African law with Delaware law, potentially disregarding the principle of *lex loci contractus* and *lex situs*, which often dictates that contracts related to property or business within a jurisdiction are governed by that jurisdiction's laws. The Law Explanation supports the notion that South African courts may disregard a chosen law if it undermines fundamental South African public policy or mandatory rules, especially regarding assets located there. Although there are no scraped snippets, the perturbation and law explanations both provide strong evidence that there is a legal contradiction. The change introduces a direct conflict with established South African conflict of laws principles.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party may disclose the information only to its officers, employees and professional advisors and then only on a strictly need -to-know basis.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party has unrestricted rights to share the information with anyone, including competitors, without limitations or obligations of confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration completely negates the core principle of a Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA), which is to restrict the dissemination of confidential information. Removing the 'need-to-know' basis and allowing unrestricted sharing directly contradicts the purpose and legal enforceability of the entire agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Law of Contract - Good Faith and Legality
perturbation.law_citation: South African contract law principles regarding good faith (bona fides) and legality.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under South African contract law, agreements must be entered into and performed in good faith (bona fides). A clause that allows unrestricted disclosure of confidential information directly undermines the fundamental purpose of the NDA and arguably violates the implied duty of good faith. Further, the clause's potential facilitation of anticompetitive behavior could render it illegal and unenforceable under South African competition law.
perturbation.justification: The original text imposes a strict 'need-to-know' basis for disclosing confidential information, while the changed text grants unrestricted rights to share the information, including with competitors, thereby completely negating the confidentiality obligations. This directly contradicts the fundamental purpose of an NDA. While no specific law snippets were provided, the perturbation explanation and law explanation highlight how this alteration undermines the core principle of an NDA and could violate the implied duty of good faith and potentially facilitate anticompetitive behavior. The change is a complete reversal of the original obligation, creating a strong legal contradiction.
